analysis:
- Top designs (Heuristics 1 & 2) share an efficient template: fast nondominated filtering → cheap per-point approximation to rank candidates → form a small deterministic shortlist → greedy selection with exact hypervolume (HV) computed only on the shortlist → bounded local improvements. They minimize expensive HV calls, cache current HV, and enforce deterministic tie-breaks and caps.
- Strong patterns: shortlisting (limits O(N) expensive work), incremental HV caching (store current_hv, selected_pts), and using cheap scalar proxies (box volume or weight-vector scalarizations) to prioritize candidates.
- Avoidable overhead in weaker methods:
  - Repeated full HV recomputations inside tight Python loops (many heuristics rebuild arrays and call pg.hypervolume per candidate per iteration).
  - Recreating pygmo hypervolume objects and vstacking arrays repeatedly instead of updating cached structures.
  - Unbounded local search or evolutionary loops that perform many HV evaluations with little pruning (TPOSS, SPESS, GLHSS).
  - Complex internal machinery (GL_HSS) that adds algorithmic complexity and Python-level overhead without clear HV-call reduction.
  - Poor vectorization: many per-point comparisons are done in nested Python loops rather than batched NumPy operations or bitset masks.
- Missed opportunities observed:
  - CELF/lazy-greedy and weight-vector approximations are present in weaker and mid heuristics but often not combined with tight HV-call budgeting; when they are combined (Heuristic 11) they still rebuild arrays and lack reuse of hypervolume objects.
  - Many heuristics could reduce calls by precomputing singleton HVs, storing marginal gains with timestamps (CELF), and updating only affected candidates after a selection/swaps.
  - Deterministic shortlists and small, well-chosen weight sets (scaled with k and d) give good quality/efficiency tradeoffs; over-large weight sets or heavy metaheuristics increase runtime with marginal HV improvement.
experience: Prefer deterministic shortlisting + a lazy-greedy core: (1) fast Pareto filter, (2) cheap vectorized scoring to pick a small candidate pool ≈ O(k)–O(k·log k), (3) CELF-style greedy using cached singleton HVs and current_hv to avoid repeated full HV recomputes, (4) bounded local swaps that only recompute HV for affected replacements. Tune shortlist size proportional to k and cache everything.
current_reflection: Prefer deterministic shortlisting + a lazy-greedy core: (1) fast Pareto filter, (2) cheap vectorized scoring to pick a small candidate pool ≈ O(k)–O(k·log k), (3) CELF-style greedy using cached singleton HVs and current_hv to avoid repeated full HV recomputes, (4) bounded local swaps that only recompute HV for affected replacements. Tune shortlist size proportional to k and cache everything.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: deterministic shortlist, Pareto filter, vectorized scoring, CELF greedy, cached singleton HVs, bounded swaps, batched HV
- Advice: Prefilter dominated points, vectorized-score with few weight vectors to shortlist ≈ c·k, run CELF greedy using cached singleton contributions plus maintained current_HV to avoid full recomputes, apply bounded local swaps recomputing only affected HV deltas; batch/cache HV calls and seed randomness for deterministic exactly‑k output.
- Avoid: Avoid repeated full HV recomputes, nested HV calls, huge shortlists, per-candidate Python loops, non-determinism, and missing incremental caches.
- Explanation: Shrinks candidates early, CELF + cached singletons minimizes HV evaluations (near O(k log k)), bounded swaps improve solution with little extra HV work — fast, deterministic, reproducible.
